video_title: MacroVoices #535 Michael Every: NAFTA and NAPTHA – Warcraft & Fartcraft
gist: Rabobank global strategist Michael Every and oil analyst Rory Johnston dissect the Strait of Hormuz blockade as a catalyst for a US shift from economic policy to 'economic statecraft,' arguing the crisis may force radical energy-market restructuring (a NAFTHA closed-loop trade bloc), Fed redesign under Kevin Warsh, and stablecoin-based dollar geopolitics. Johnston provides granular oil-flow data showing 13–15 mb/d shut in, Chinese crude imports halved to ~6 mb/d, and buffers drawing at the fastest pace on record, yet prices remain below $100 because speculators have been scared out by Trump's Truth Social jawboning.

themes.theme: Strait of Hormuz supply shock: scale, buffers, and price paradox

insights.claim: Approximately 13–15 million barrels per day of oil production remains shut in because trapped vessels in the Persian Gulf cannot export, constituting the core supply crisis from the Hormuz closure.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: This is the largest supply shock ever recorded; any resumption of trade or deal timeline directly drives the magnitude and duration of price upside.
insights.quote: That 13 to 15 million barrel a day is the core supply crisis or production crisis really that is currently still gripping the oil market and crossings out of the straight are not going to ameliate that issue.

insights.claim: Roughly 2,000 vessels are trapped in the Persian Gulf, of which 800 are large merchant ships; only 1 to ~12 ships per day have been crossing, slowly draining what is effectively a captive strategic petroleum reserve of trapped oil.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Each ship crossing is a marginal release of dead inventory, not new production; the ceiling on this relief flow is physically constrained and far too small to offset the shut-in barrels.
insights.quote: We still have hundreds of vessels trapped in the Gulf, uh the Persian Gulf, the Arabian Gulf... 2,000 vessels or so. Now 800 of those are the large merchant ships.

insights.claim: Even if a Hormuz deal is signed immediately, clearing stranded ships and restarting shut-in production would take weeks to months before oil flows normalize, meaning the supply shock has a multi-month tail regardless of the diplomatic outcome.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: A peace deal headline should not be treated as an immediate oil supply relief event; logistical timelines mean prices stay elevated for months post-deal.
insights.quote: there will be a process that will need to be started of clearing long stranded ships out of the Gulf and then getting new ships into the Gulf. And that process is going to take weeks and more likely months before you can even start the monthsl long process of restarting oil production that's been shut in.

insights.claim: Global commercial oil inventories are drawing down at the fastest pace of any crisis on record, yet crude prices were below $100/bbl (WTI ~$96, Brent ~$98) at the time of recording, a historically anomalous divergence.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: The draw rate implies an eventual violent price correction; the disconnect signals either structural market changes or a hidden buffer (China's invisible SPR) that makes timing uncertain.
insights.quote: We are now drawing that down at the fastest pace of any crisis on record. If that was happening in any other moment in the market, the market would be way more panicked than it is right now.

insights.claim: Speculator washout caused by Trump's Truth Social jawboning is a primary reason oil prices have not reacted proportionally to the supply shock; presidential tweets have knocked $20+ from crude in under 10 minutes, scaring systematic traders out of long positions.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Once physical inventory draws exhaust buffers, no tweet can offset real-market rebalancing; this creates an asymmetric long setup once the speculator clearing is complete.
insights.quote: they learned the power of the truth social post, which is Trump can knock $20 plus out of the price of crude oil in 10 minutes... the speculators got knocked out of this market

insights.claim: Rory Johnston's updated base case is oil reaching ~$150/bbl if the Strait of Hormuz remains substantially closed through Labor Day (early September 2026); maintaining sub-$100 oil through that timeline would require him to 'rip up the entire way I thought I understood the market.'
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Provides a credible, data-grounded price target and timeline; the $150 level represents a physical-rebalancing floor, not a speculative spike.
insights.quote: I don't see a way to avoid substantially higher prices. 150 kind of would be my updated assumption around what that would be like on that timeline.

insights.claim: Dr. Anas Alhajji argued the effective net supply deficit is only ~3–4 mb/d rather than 13 mb/d because mitigations (demand response, inventory drawdowns, rerouting) have offset most of the gross shut-in, and he expects oil below $100 in 2027 with prices peaking in 2026.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: The most bullish (least fearful) credible bear case from an oil-market veteran still implies a spike to ~$150 before a 2026 peak — the bull floor is high even in the benign scenario.
insights.quote: it's only about 3 or 4 million barrels, not 13, that are a deficit in the net... He thinks oil prices below 100 in 27, but maybe uh they're not done going up in 26.

insights.claim: The Strait of Bab-el-Mandeb closure by Houthi proxies is identified as a key tail-risk accelerant: much of the oil blocked from Hormuz is being re-routed via Saudi pipeline to the Red Sea, so closing Bab-el-Mandeb would effectively shut the last meaningful relief valve.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Monitor Israel-Lebanon escalation as a leading indicator for Houthi re-engagement; a Bab-el-Mandeb closure would collapse the pipeline workaround and produce an extreme price shock.
insights.quote: if that gets closed too and it is well within the possibility of the Houthis and Iran's proxies that could definitely escalate things and escalate things and escalate them very quickly.

themes.theme: China's hidden oil balance: invisible SPR and geopolitical optionality

insights.claim: Chinese crude oil imports collapsed approximately 50% from pre-war levels (~12 mb/d seaborne) to ~6 mb/d, representing a volume roughly equal to half the total Hormuz supply shock — yet Chinese economic activity data (trucking, road congestion, flights) shows no corresponding demand destruction.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: China is drawing from invisible (underground or unreported) strategic reserves, not suffering economic damage; this creates enormous uncertainty about how long China can sustain low imports and when the buying impulse reverses.
insights.quote: Chinese crude oil imports have fallen by roughly 50%. Relative to prewar levels. Prior to that in terms of seaborn imports, they were importing ballparks 12 million barrels a day of crude. Now that you know as of the latest uh you know four-week average, we're looking at 6 million barrels a day of crude.

insights.claim: Chinese refinery runs have collapsed even as domestic fuel consumption data (mobility, trucking) remains stable, implying China is releasing refined product from invisible stockpiles rather than processing new crude — likely built up during the 2023 'demand surge' that was actually strategic pre-war stockpiling.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: China's visible refining collapse is a signal of strategic inventory deployment, not economic weakness; when those refined-product stocks deplete, refining and crude imports will surge simultaneously, tightening global balances sharply.
insights.quote: One of the theories at the time was that it wasn't real demand at all. It was it was strategic stockpiling of refined products potentially in anticipation of a war or invasion of Taiwan.

insights.claim: Chinese domestic fuel retail prices were capped at a level implying only ~30% price increase versus a global doubling, crushing refinery margins to deeply negative levels and incentivizing a halt to production and export of refined products.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Price-cap policy provides a purely commercial (not just geopolitical) explanation for China's refinery run collapse; lifting the cap or deep negative margins eventually forces Beijing to either raise prices or boost refining subsidies.
insights.quote: Chinese basically fuel cap fuel price caps on pump prices for retail consumers have dramatically shielded China Chinese consumers from the explosion in global prices. Like you've seen a rise in I think Beijing's um retail prices for gasoline of like maybe 30% versus a global doubling.

insights.claim: China's pullback from crude purchasing directly freed up incremental supply for Korean, Japanese, and Australian importers, enabling those US-allied Asian economies to cushion their own supply shock; Johnston interprets this as China positioning itself as a 'multilateral good-guy' in Asia as US credibility under Trump erodes.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Beijing is running a soft-power play via oil market behavior; watch Chinese import levels as a leading indicator of whether China is tightening or loosening its regional influence campaign.
insights.quote: I think part of that is that they could be trying to step into a bit more of a multilateral governance good guy role in a part of the world that now sees Trump as someone who left them for slaughter.

insights.claim: Satellite-based inventory trackers (Kepler) show China holds well over 1 billion barrels of visible crude oil inventories, which continued rising through most of the Hormuz crisis — the drawdowns are coming from underground or statistically invisible stocks that cannot be monitored.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: The opacity of China's true reserve levels is the single largest unresolvable uncertainty in the global oil market; any intelligence on underground storage status would be highly market-moving.
insights.quote: we do have up until you know recent crisis a pretty unprecedented degree of visibility into global at least inventory of crude oil... we know that China has well over a billion barrels of crude oil across the country

insights.claim: Johnston's key forward trigger: when Chinese crude imports begin rising again from the current 5–6 mb/d floor while Hormuz is still closed, that combination of tighter realized balances (drawn inventories) plus renewed Chinese demand will catalyze the next large oil price rip — likely by end of June to July 2026.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Watch Chinese weekly crude import data as the primary leading indicator for the next oil price spike; the trigger is the delta turning from loosening to tightening.
insights.quote: when the delta and the impulse shifts from loosening back to tightening. I think that's probably when we do that next rip higher

insights.claim: Michael Every raised the scenario that China, if its strategic reserves are as depleted as they may actually be, could be forced to cooperate with the US against Iran — accepting geostrategic damage to its Iranian alliance to avoid catastrophic domestic energy disruption.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A China-US backchannel deal on Hormuz would be the ultimate bull-killer for oil prices; watch for any unusual diplomatic signaling between Washington and Beijing on energy.
insights.quote: part of this resolution could be, ironically, China having to work with the US against its own ally, Iran, because it realizes that the potential damage to it from this crisis is even larger than losing Iran as an ally.

themes.theme: Economic statecraft: NAFTHA, swap lines, and the dollar architecture shift

insights.claim: Michael Every has argued since Trump's re-election that the US would shift from 'economic policy' to 'economic statecraft,' and the US government is now explicitly using that phrase publicly — the Iran crisis is accelerating this transition.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: The framing shift has direct implications for how every instrument of US power — Fed, Treasury, dollar, military — is coordinated; investors pricing markets as if the old neoliberal policy framework persists are mispricing regime risk.
insights.quote: the US is openly now saying publicly that economic statecraft is what it's pursuing. And that's absolutely part and parcel of the argument I was just making then about this energy crisis

insights.claim: Every proposed a 'NAFTHA' scenario — a North American Petroleum and Hydrocarbons Trading Association — where the US creates a closed-loop energy bloc with Mexico, Canada, Venezuela, Guyana, key Latin American producers, select Middle Eastern partners (UAE), and refining-capable US-allied Asian economies, allowing these countries to weather the Hormuz crisis while the rest of the world faces catastrophic shortages.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: If implemented, NAFTHA would bifurcate global oil pricing along geopolitical lines, making energy access a function of alliance status — a structurally higher risk premium for non-US-aligned importers.
insights.quote: Instead of having NAFTA, you have NAFTA, which is the North American Petroleum and Hydrocarbons Trading Hub Association where effectively you have again this closed loop for energy

insights.claim: Dollar swap lines are being weaponized as economic statecraft: the Fed has offered swap lines to the UAE (which has a dollar-pegged currency, large energy reserves, and recently left OPEC+), signaling a shift from swap lines as an Old Boys' Club financial stability tool to a quid-pro-quo geopolitical reward for physical-commodity allies.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Countries that align with US energy statecraft gain access to the ultimate financial backstop; those that don't lose dollar liquidity insurance — a massive asymmetry favoring US-aligned commodity producers.
insights.quote: swap lines... have now been offered to the UAE which have is itself very cashri has a currency pegged to the dollar and has an awful lot of energy and is no longer in OPEC and OPEC plus

insights.claim: Kevin Warsh himself stated publicly that Fed swap lines are not subject to Fed independence because they are geopolitical transactions — giving the presidency and Treasury de facto control over swap line allocation.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Warsh's framing legally and institutionally opens the door for the White House to direct swap line policy; this undermines the traditional non-political use of swap lines and could be used as leverage in trade and energy negotiations.
insights.quote: Walsh when he was asked what do you think about dollar swap lines... He gave an interesting answer where he alluded to the fact that he thought Fed independence didn't apply to them because they were a geopolitical transaction

insights.claim: The US stablecoin strategy serves as a 'buy one get one free' dollar extension tool: countries willing to align with US tariff policy, technology supply chain separation from China, and defense cooperation will accept dollar-pegged stablecoins, which give the US free seigniorage and sort allies from adversaries.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Stablecoin adoption by allied nations is a proxy indicator for deepening US economic alignment; companies building stablecoin infrastructure for cross-border settlement in allied nations are positioned at the intersection of finance and geopolitics.
insights.quote: they also have a sorting effect and that the countries that will trade with you that will put tariffs on China and share a common external tariff with you against them and split their technology supply chains... They will be the ones who will accept them

insights.claim: Canada's rhetoric shifted dramatically from Prime Minister Carney saying in January 2026 'we're in favor of a new world order working with China' to saying in late May 2026 'we want to form a new partnership with the US to make America great again' — Every attributes this to the coercive power of the looming energy crisis logic.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that economic statecraft coercion is already reshaping allied country positions without military action; similar pivots could emerge from other wavering allies under energy-crisis pressure.
insights.quote: you had the prime minister, you know, Carney saying that we're in favor of a new world order working with China... to last week saying we want to form a new partnership with the US to make America great again. And that's a direct quote.

themes.theme: Federal Reserve transformation under Kevin Warsh

insights.claim: Every argues that an independent central bank targeting 2% CPI is logically incompatible with a state pursuing economic statecraft; he expects the Fed under Warsh, who was backed for the role by Bessant and Trump, to gradually align with the statecraft agenda.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Markets that price Fed policy as an independent technocratic process are missing the regime shift; rate and QE decisions may increasingly reflect geopolitical objectives rather than inflation targeting alone.
insights.quote: The Fed will from my particular perspective of global strategy gradually be moving more towards the economic statecraft field

insights.claim: Every anticipates the Warsh Fed may introduce sector-specific interest rates or balance sheet support — e.g., lower rates or QE for physical-economy sectors (mining, ports, shipbuilding, military supply chains) while withdrawing support from financial-sector assets.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A differentiated-rate or sectoral-QE framework would be a radical structural break for financial markets; sectors tied to physical-economy reindustrialization would outperform financial-asset-heavy sectors under this regime.
insights.quote: The Fed might be having to think right do we really need to be offering QE style balance sheet support to the financial part of the economy which effectively doesn't do anything in terms of actual physical bang for the buck... do we need to be looking at some kind of acronym tacit balance sheet support... for the physical economy

insights.claim: There is a fundamental contradiction in Warsh's stated agenda: he wants to reduce the Fed balance sheet while Trump wants to expand the Pentagon budget by $500 billion next year — Every frames this as an unresolved square-circle problem.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: The fiscal-monetary tension implies either the Fed balance sheet reduction fails (bullish risk assets, dollar negative) or the defense expansion is unfunded (bearish bonds), with limited room for both simultaneously.
insights.quote: Walsh is openly talking about trying to reduce the Fed balance sheet which itself would really set the cat among the pigeons... at the same time Trump is talking about expanding the Pentagon budget next year by $500 billion well how do you square that circle

insights.claim: Every's broader critique of central banking: existing models were built for demand-management and cannot process structural supply-side shocks; he compares the Fed's fitness for current conditions to 'a fish understanding a bicycle,' meaning no central bank rate policy is the correct solution to an energy supply shock.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Investors should not rely on central bank intervention to stabilize markets during the Hormuz crisis; the policy backstop that has historically capped downside does not apply to supply-side structural shocks.
insights.quote: like central banks understand supply side or structural supply side shocks like a fish understands a bicycle. They're just entirely different things.

insights.claim: Warsh's public statements before taking the Fed chair position focused on AI being very deflationary, implying rate cuts ahead — making the Iran supply-side inflation shock a direct intellectual curveball for his stated thesis.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A Warsh Fed caught between AI-deflation thesis (dovish) and energy-supply inflation (hawkish) may produce erratic, contradictory signals — watch for unusual Fed communication inconsistency as a regime-transition signal.
insights.quote: he wanted to change the Fed... looking at the fact that AI in his mind from in public statements is likely to be very deflationary and as a result, you know, there's room for the rate cuts there

themes.theme: Geopolitical outcomes: Iran 'loss' scenarios and US credibility

insights.claim: Every defines a US 'loss' in the Iran conflict as a 'taco' (full retreat) — not military defeat but geostrategic capitulation where Trump withdraws the blockade, leaving Iran controlling Hormuz; he frames this as a potential 1956-Suez-Crisis-level demotion for the US from hyperpower to merely 'a power.'
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A US tactical retreat would reprice geopolitical risk premiums globally, reduce confidence in US security guarantees to Gulf allies, and likely push them toward Iranian accommodation — structurally bullish energy prices and bearish US dollar credibility.
insights.quote: what it would do at a meta level is show that even when you have a very ostensibly gung-ho president like Trump... the US would be in a state that uh the geostrategist Edward Ludback refers to as posh heroic

insights.claim: A US retreat from Hormuz would likely force Gulf states (UAE, Saudi Arabia) to negotiate terms with Iran despite hostility, but not unconditionally — UAE and Israel would likely continue covert operations, meaning structurally high energy prices could persist even post-retreat.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Even the 'benign' US retreat scenario doesn't produce clean energy-price normalization; the Gulf remains contested, maintaining a geopolitical risk premium in oil.
insights.quote: Countries that are permanently based there may not accept the fact that that happens and say we're not going to give up

insights.claim: Every argues the US is 'post-heroic' (casualty-phobic) in the Luttwak sense — possessing a technologically superior but politically constrained military that cannot achieve all its geostrategic objectives, as demonstrated by inability to decisively defeat Iran without ground troops that have no domestic political support.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Investors should assign non-trivial probability to US de-escalation or retreat even under Trump; the administration's military maximalism is constrained by casualty-aversion, limiting the upside of betting on full military resolution.
insights.quote: the US is so casualty phobic that while it has this magnificent technological military machine... It's limited in what it can achieve. It can do a lot, but it can't achieve everything it needs to.

insights.claim: Trump's Labor Day comment ('the blockade could be lifted as soon as Labor Day') is treated by Both Johnston and Townsend as a highly significant tell — confirming a 3-month timeline for the crisis baseline, not an imminent resolution.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Trump's own framing gives the crisis a ~95-day runway (to Labor Day ~Sept 1, 2026), over which inventory buffers will be seriously depleted — pricing should be building in a mid-Q3 2026 crunch.
insights.quote: President Trump even made the comment that the US blockade of Iran could be lifted as soon as Labor Day. As soon as Labor Day. That's 95 days from now, more than a quarter of a year away.

insights.claim: Iran's consistent position is maintaining 'nuclear threshold state' status — possessing enriched uranium sufficient to build a weapon if desired but not having made that decision — while the US/Israel demand surrender of enriched material; this fundamental gap makes a near-term deal structurally unlikely.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Trump's repeated 'Iran agreed to no nukes' tweets are not new information; the sticking point is enriched uranium ownership, not a weapons-program pledge — traders taking those tweets as deal signals are being deliberately misled.
insights.quote: Iran agreed to no nuclear weapon years ago. That hasn't changed... What they've said consistently is they do not have nor do they intend to develop a nuclear weapon. However, they are not willing to give up their enriched uranium

insights.claim: Gold has been anomalously weak during the Iran conflict — failing to rally on missile strikes — which Every partially explains by Middle Eastern high-net-worth individuals selling gold for dollars to flee the region, finding fiat currency more portable in an active war zone than physical metals.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: The gold weakness is partly structural (war-zone liquidation) not just macro (rising yields/dollar); this selling pressure dissipates post-conflict, which could be a catalyst for gold's recovery.
insights.quote: people were finding that they were having to sell gold for dollars to try and then move them abroad and then buy other assets again. So when it actually came push came to shove in the Middle East you actually had missiles flying in a region which historically likes gold a lot.

insights.claim: Patrick Szersna identifies gold's 200-day moving average at ~$4,415 as the critical technical support; if that level breaks, he expects an accelerated move lower and does not see a catalyst for gold bulls until Q3 2026 at the earliest.
insights.speaker: Patrick Szersna
insights.so_what: Technical and macro headwinds (strengthening dollar, rising yields) together with war-liquidation selling argue for staying underweight gold until the Iran resolution clarifies the geopolitical architecture.
insights.quote: So far, the 200 day moving average has held when we saw that test right around 4,400... I won't be surprised though if the Iran situation continues to persist if that does not hold.

insights.claim: Every argues the dollar cannot be abandoned by the US because the eurodollar system grants enormous power, but that same system de-industrialized the US by incentivizing financial asset accumulation over productive capacity — the statecraft agenda is to reform the dollar's structure while retaining its global role.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: The dollar is not being abandoned but re-engineered; investors should watch for instruments that extend dollar reach into physical-commodity networks (stablecoins, new swap lines) rather than just financial assets.
insights.quote: clearly there's absolutely no appetite from Bess or from Trump to give up the global power of the dollar. But there's a full understanding that the Euro dollar system... is also responsible for de-industrializing it

insights.claim: The S&P 500 rallied almost 20% trough-to-peak in roughly 2 months as of early June 2026, reaching ~7,553; Szersna flags the RSI as 'incredibly overbought' on short-term metrics with only four comparable rallies in 20 years, and warns of poor risk/reward for adding longs.
insights.speaker: Patrick Szersna
insights.so_what: Equity markets are pricing a benign oil/Iran outcome while commodity experts see a structural crisis; oil breaking above $100 is flagged as a likely catalyst for equity distribution/correction.
insights.quote: Going back uh almost 20 years, there's only four times we've seen a rally of this magnitude. And certainly, you know, the market is stretched on almost every metric.

insights.claim: 10-year US Treasury yields were at 4.48% and appearing to have a path-of-least-resistance toward a 5% handle, with oil and inflation expectations driving the correlation; there is no identified bond-buying opportunity at current levels.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: If oil breaks above $100, inflation expectations re-accelerate and could push the 10-year back to or above 5%, compressing equity multiples and increasing fiscal stress on the large US deficit.
insights.quote: can we see a 5% handle on the 10-year yield. It's certainly at this point uh the path of least resistance the way these charts look at this stage.

insights.claim: Copper was trading at 52-week highs (~$6.50/lb) with old dips being bought; Szersna identifies copper as the most bullish commodity chart with a potential run toward $7, consistent with industrial-reindustrialization demand themes.
insights.speaker: Patrick Szersna
insights.so_what: Copper's strength while oil is contained suggests markets are positioning for physical-economy rebuild (infrastructure, defense, EVs) independent of the Iran resolution; long copper is an expression of the statecraft/re-industrialization thesis.
insights.quote: copper markets, which continued to trade at 52- week highs... will be interesting to see whether or not copper can break to that fresh new high and make a gun for the seven level on the upside.

insights.claim: Uranium was quiet and failing to participate in the broader commodity rally despite a pop on the Nuclear Fuels Conference that fully retraced; seasonally weak until late August, with a potential catalyst at the WNA conference (September 7–11, 2026).
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Long-term uranium bull thesis intact but near-term seasonal weakness and failure to participate in commodity rally suggests waiting for WNA conference as a re-entry catalyst.
insights.quote: seasonally, this is a weak period until late August or so. Maybe we'll hold steady, but I don't think we should expect a big rally or all-time highs or any of that until we get into the seasonal buying period, which normally starts right around the WNA conference

themes.theme: Physical-economy rebuild trade: PAVE ETF construction

insights.claim: Patrick Szersna's trade-of-the-week operationalizes Every's physical-economy rebuild thesis via a long position in the PAVE ETF (~$57.24) paired with a July 17, 2026 $55 put at ~$1.25 as a short-term hedge; total effective cost basis ~$58.49, defined short-term floor at $55 (~6% max risk through expiry).
insights.speaker: Patrick Szersna
insights.so_what: Provides a specific, actionable structure to express the re-industrialization theme with capped near-term downside given an overbought market; the $55 strike aligns with the 50-day moving average (~$55.06) as a natural technical support.
insights.quote: I'm looking at the July 17th, 2026 $55 put, which has roughly 43 days to expiration, which is trading around $1.25. That strike sits almost directly on the 50-day moving average currently at 5506

insights.claim: Every's and Szersna's shared thesis is that policy is shifting from broad financial-asset QE support to targeted support for infrastructure, ports, shipbuilding, mining, supply chains, and strategic manufacturing — an 'entire generation of financial market participants' has never experienced a market where liquidity flows selectively to some sectors and not others.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Portfolios built for the 2010s QE regime (financial assets, tech, growth stocks as primary beneficiaries of Fed liquidity) face structural underperformance if selective sectoral liquidity becomes the new normal.
insights.quote: I don't think people have in markets broadly got their heads around what it would mean to have extra liquidity that goes to some sectors, but it doesn't flow to all the favored ones, which is what an entire generation, if not two, of financial market participants have grown up expecting as normal.
